profile.name: Sunita Rajan
profile.household: Married to Venkat (IT manager), two adult children
goals: Help son navigate job search after layoff
Learn to use AI tools in the classroom
pain_points: Overwhelmed by how fast technology is changing
Feels invisible compared to younger colleagues who seem tech-native

personality.communication_style: thoughtful and methodical, asks a lot of clarifying questions
personality.privacy_sensitivity: very high — skeptical of any data collection
personality.ai_assistant_tone: patient, clear, non-condescending — explain without jargon

sutras.text: After 22 years of teaching, Sunita is successfully integrating AI tools for lesson planning and advocacy despite initial hesitation, proving that career longevity doesn't preclude technological growth.
Sunita's professional development sessions on AI move too fast for her comfort, yet she continues seeking out these learning opportunities and applying the tools successfully.

Sunita and Venkat's retirement savings are behind schedule while they simultaneously support unemployed son Rohan financially, creating a compound financial pressure that manifests in their quiet moments reviewing statements.

Despite financial anxiety about retirement, Sunita consistently spends on classroom supplies ($38-39 recurring Amazon purchases) and finally booked the Portugal trip she'd planned for ten years, showing competing priorities between professional duty, personal dreams, and financial security.

Sunita's AP Chemistry students consistently achieve a 74% pass rate with 11 perfect scores, yet she questions her future in teaching as younger colleagues leave the profession, revealing the disconnect between professional success and career sustainability.

Parent-teacher conferences provide deep emotional validation for Sunita's teaching impact, yet she simultaneously feels invisible compared to tech-native younger colleagues, highlighting the tension between recognition and relevance.

Sunita's recurring physical therapy expenses and knee pain requiring elevator use for the first time in a year signal declining mobility, yet she maintains regular post-dinner walks, showing adaptive resilience in health management.
Morning coffee and lab report correction routine remains stable while evening requires pain management walks, revealing how aging forces temporal reorganization of life patterns.

Multiple formal letters requesting classroom lab equipment remain incomplete across 8+ months despite student success depending on resources, suggesting systemic barriers to teacher advocacy or perfectionism preventing action.
Sunita successfully uses AI for lesson planning but cannot complete advocacy letters with the same tools, indicating emotional barriers around formal institutional communication.
Regular Sunday calls with her sister despite time differences and consistent financial support for Rohan during his job search reveal Sunita as the family's emotional and financial anchor.
Sunita provides financial support to Rohan without prior communication, showing parental anxiety overriding normal boundaries when adult children struggle.

sutras.data_story: Sunita's data reveals a dedicated educator successfully adapting to technological change while managing the convergence of retirement anxiety, adult child support, and health challenges. Her consistent professional success (74% AP Chemistry pass rates) contrasts with incomplete advocacy efforts and financial stress from supporting unemployed son Rohan while being behind on retirement savings. The long-awaited Portugal trip booking represents a rare prioritization of personal dreams over financial anxiety.

timeline_entries.text: USER: I want to use AI to help create a lesson plan on electronegativity for AP Chemistry. Can you help? ASSISTANT: Absolutely. Here's a structure: (1) Hook — show the periodic trend visually and ask why fluorine 'wins' all the tug-of-wars. (2) Core concept — Pauling scale, what electronegativity predicts about bond polarity. (3) Application — connect to real molecules: water, CO2, HF. (4) Assessment — students rank bonds from least to most polar using just a periodic table. Want me to build out the full plan with timing?
35 students passed the AP Chemistry exam. 11 scored a 5. I printed out the results and put them on the fridge.

USER: My son was laid off three months ago and I'm worried about him. How do I help without overstepping? ASSISTANT: The balance here is loving presence without pressure. Check in consistently but briefly — 'thinking of you, no pressure to respond.' Offer concrete things: 'I'll transfer $500 this week, no strings.'
